1.synthesis: God tells Moses that he has hardened Pharaoh's heart so that these signs might be told to future generations: 'that you may know that I am the Lord' (vv.1–2). Moses warns Pharaoh of a locust swarm unprecedented in Egyptian history: they will cover the earth so that it cannot be seen, and will devour every plant left by the hail (vv.3–6). Even Pharaoh's own servants urge him: 'Do you not yet understand that Egypt is ruined?' (v.7). Pharaoh recalls Moses but offers only the men — the women, children, and livestock must stay (vv.8–11). Moses stretches out his rod; an east wind blows all day and night and brings the locusts at morning (vv.12–15); they fill every house and cover the land. Pharaoh again confesses and begs intercession; a mighty west wind drives all locusts into the Red Sea, and Pharaoh's heart is once more hardened (vv.16–20).
1.historical_context: Adam Clarke quotes eyewitness accounts from travelers — Volney, Baron de Tott, Dr. Shaw — describing locust swarms that covered the earth 'for several leagues, to the depth of six or seven inches,' darkened the sun, and stripped every green thing in hours. The Hebrew name arbeh derives from rabah, 'to multiply,' reflecting their defining characteristic. Keil and Delitzsch note that the east wind bringing locusts from Arabia rather than the usual south wind proved God's power extended far beyond Egypt's borders. The broken negotiation of verses 8–11 — Pharaoh's insistence that only the men may go — reveals his real concern: if the families and livestock depart, Israel will never return. Calvin notes the bitter irony that Egypt, saved by Joseph's wisdom from famine two and a half centuries earlier, is now stripped of its own harvest by God's judgment.
1.literary_structure: The eighth plague carries an explicit purpose-statement at its opening (vv.1–2) that shifts the frame from immediate judgment to transgenerational testimony: the whole plague-series is for the instruction of Israel's children and grandchildren. The locusts attack what the hail left (v.5, v.12, v.15), creating cumulative total destruction of Egypt's agricultural base. The court crisis (v.7: 'Egypt is destroyed') signals that even Pharaoh's inner circle has broken from its earlier solidarity with his stubbornness. Pharaoh's temporary confession and request for intercession (v.17: 'pray to the Lord your God only to remove this death from me') pointedly asks only for removal of the plague, not for forgiveness or genuine submission — Matthew Henry calls this 'counterfeit repentance.'
1.theological_application: Matthew Henry: 'Who then is able to stand before the great God? A formidable army of horse and foot might more easily have been resisted than this host of insects.' He draws the lesson: 'Let our labour be, not for the habitation and meat which lie thus exposed, but for those which endure to eternal life, which cannot be thus invaded nor thus corrupted' (John 6:27). Calvin on the irony: 'it is worth remarking the nature of the scourge — God collects and arms a host of vile insects whereby He may insultingly overcome this indomitable tyrant with all his forces.' Gill on Pharaoh's retraction of his offer after the plague is removed: the reprobate make promises under the press of judgment that dissolve as soon as the pressure lifts. Matthew Henry on the court's counsel: even Pharaoh's servants recognized that God was prevailing, but Pharaoh's pride overrode every counsel.
1.christology: Moses's intercession after Pharaoh's confession (v.17–18) removes the consuming plague from the land: a west wind carries every locust into the Red Sea. This intercessory act prefigures the mediatorial work of Christ, whose intercession (Romans 8:34; Hebrews 7:25) turns aside divine judgment from those under his care. The locusts consuming what the hail left anticipates the eschatological locusts of Revelation 9:3–10, which are kept from harming those who have the seal of God on their foreheads — the same distinction between Israel and Egypt enacted here: those under God's covering are kept; those outside are consumed.

21.synthesis: 'Then the Lord said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand toward heaven, that there may be darkness over the land of Egypt, a darkness to be felt." So Moses stretched out his hand toward heaven, and there was pitch darkness in all the land of Egypt three days. They did not see one another, nor did anyone rise from his place for three days, but all the people of Israel had light where they lived' (vv.21–23). Pharaoh summons Moses and proposes a final compromise: all the people may go, but the livestock must remain. Moses refuses — the livestock must come for sacrifice and burnt offering, 'not a hoof shall be left behind' (v.26). God hardens Pharaoh's heart; he drives Moses out with a death-threat: 'The day you see my face you shall die' (v.28). Moses accepts the finality: 'As you say! I will not see your face again' (v.29).
21.historical_context: The plague of darkness was a direct theological assault on Ra, the supreme sun-god of Egypt, and on Atum the creator-deity whose primary manifestation was solar. Three days of palpable, immobilizing darkness rendered the Egyptians' chief object of worship utterly powerless. Adam Clarke notes that the darkness was so thick it 'might be felt' — possibly an intensified khamsin sandstorm raised to miraculous degree, or a darkness with no natural analogy whatsoever. Israel having light in Goshen while all Egypt was immobilized mirrors the pattern of all previous Goshen exemptions, now made maximally dramatic. The livestock dispute (vv.24–26) reveals Pharaoh's strategy: keep the cattle as collateral to guarantee Israel's return. Moses's refusal is based not on bargaining but on theological principle — the Lord himself will designate which animals are to be offered, and that cannot be determined until they reach the appointed place of worship.
21.literary_structure: Like the sixth plague (boils), the ninth is sent without prior warning, suggesting a further contraction of the space given to Pharaoh's repentance. The plague falls with absolute simplicity: Moses stretches out his hand and the darkness arrives. The three-day duration creates a sustained crisis that, unlike the fleeting shock of previous plagues, forces Egypt into total paralysis. The livestock negotiation of verses 24–26 is the last attempt at a partial concession; Moses's 'not a hoof shall be left behind' closes off all compromise. The death-threat of verse 28 and Moses's response in verse 29 formally end the negotiation phase; the following chapter (11:4–8) is Moses's final speech of judgment, continuous with this scene.
21.theological_application: Matthew Henry on the plague's significance: 'God shows men their sin in their punishment; as the Egyptians had kept Israel in the darkness of slavery, now God kept them in literal darkness.' Adam Clarke: 'The Egyptians were hereby convinced that Jehovah alone was the sovereign — their sun-god was helpless.' Gill on Pharaoh's livestock demand: 'He was well acquainted with the purposes of Moses, that they intended a final departure, and not a temporary absence; therefore he sought to retain a pledge.' Moses's refusal — 'not a hoof shall be left behind' — embodies the principle that worship cannot be partial: God requires the whole person, the whole household, the whole flock. Matthew Henry: 'Those that serve God must serve him with all they have.'
21.christology: The three days of darkness over Egypt while Israel has light becomes, in the New Testament's retrospective typology, a vivid anticipation of the three hours of darkness at Golgotha (Matthew 27:45) — the moment when the Light of the world passed through the darkness of judgment so that his people might never perish in it. The plague of darkness enacts visibly what John declares conceptually: 'The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it' (John 1:5). Israel's light in Goshen while Egypt is immobilized prefigures the community of those who walk in the light of Christ while the world lies in darkness (1 John 5:19).
